title: Hypertension — Essential
oneLiner: Chronic elevation of systemic arterial pressure (≥140/90 mmHg) without a single identifiable cause; affects ~1 in 3 adults; major preventable risk for stroke, MI, kidney failure.

sections.Essential Hypertension — Study Mode: **One-liner:** Chronic elevation of systemic arterial pressure (≥140/90 mmHg) without a single identifiable cause; affects ~1 in 3 adults; major preventable risk for stroke, MI, kidney failure.
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 52-year-old man attends a routine health screen. He is asymptomatic, works as a taxi driver, smokes 20/day, and drinks 6 units of alcohol nightly. His BMI is 30. Blood pressure is 162/98 in both arms; his heart rate is 78 bpm. Cardiorespiratory exam is normal, no renal bruit, fundi show no papilloedema. Urine dipstick – nil. He has no known comorbidities. You check his previous records: BP was 145/90 two years ago but not rechecked.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
- **Asymptomatic** → essential hypertension is usually silent; symptoms only appear with target-organ damage or very high readings.
- **Raised BP in both arms** → excludes coarctation when difference is absent (≥20 mmHg suggests coarctation).
- **Obesity, smoking, alcohol** → classic lifestyle drivers; essential hypertension clusters with metabolic syndrome.
- **Normal fundi** → no hypertensive retinopathy (yet); this is chronic, not acute emergency.
- **No renal bruit** → unlikely renovascular hypertension.
- **Normal urine dip** → no glomerular disease or proteinuria.
- **Previous borderline readings** → confirms chronicity; this is essential (primary) hypertension, not an acute stress response.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Malignant hypertension** – young woman (20s–30s) with headache, blurred vision, BP 230/130, acute kidney injury, papilloedema. This is NOT essential disease; it’s a hypertensive emergency requiring immediate IV therapy.
- **Labile hypertension** – patient with normal clinic BP but very high home readings (or vice versa). May be white-coat effect or masked hypertension (ambulatory BP monitoring needed).
- **Isolated systolic hypertension** – elderly patient with systolic >140 but diastolic <90. Often stiff arteries due to atherosclerosis; still requires treatment to prevent stroke.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Essential (or primary) hypertension is a sustained increase in the pressure of blood against artery walls that has no single, reversible medical cause. It results from a complex interplay of genetics, sodium intake, age, and lifestyle factors over years. It is by far the most common type of hypertension (90–95% of cases).

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** 1 in 3 adults in the UK has hypertension; prevalence rises with age to >60% over 60 years. It is the most common chronic condition in primary care.
- **Stakes:** Untreated hypertension is the single largest modifiable risk factor for stroke, myocardial infarction, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease. It kills silently.
- **Common pitfalls:** (1) Failing to measure BP correctly – patient should be seated, arm supported, cuff size appropriate; (2) diagnosing from one reading – needs confirmation over visits; (3) stopping treatment once BP “normalises” – hypertension is chronic, medication is usually lifelong; (4) ignoring secondary causes in young or treatment-resistant patients.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:**  
Essential hypertension is a slow, multi-system failure of blood pressure regulation. Think of it as the arterial tree “hardening” and volume control becoming less efficient.

- **Cardiac output (CO) and systemic vascular resistance (SVR)** – Blood pressure = CO × SVR. In early essential hypertension, CO is often normal or slightly elevated (sympathetic overdrive). Over time, the kidneys fail to excrete sodium properly, leading to subtle volume expansion. Arterioles remodel and vasoconstrict (increased SVR). This pushes BP up.

- **Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS)** – In essential hypertension, the RAAS is often inappropriately active for the salt intake. High renin → angiotensin II (powerful vasoconstrictor and stimulator of aldosterone) → sodium and water retention. The kidney’s pressure-natriuresis curve shifts right: you need higher BP to excrete the same sodium load.

- **Sympathetic nervous system** – Overactivity (from stress, obesity, sleep apnoea) causes direct vasoconstriction and tachycardia. This explains why some patients have a high heart rate and why lifestyle modification helps.

- **Endothelial dysfunction** – Nitric oxide production decreases; vessels lose their ability to dilate. This contributes to the increased SVR and explains why antihypertensives that enhance NO (like ACE inhibitors) work.

- **Sodium and volume** – High dietary sodium → increased plasma volume → higher cardiac output → chronic adaptation: autoregulation increases SVR to normalise flow at higher pressure. Once established, the hypertension is volume-dependent in some patients (salt-sensitive).

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Elevated BP on measurement** → direct result of increased SVR and/or CO.
- **Usually asymptomatic** → the body adapts slowly; organ damage takes years.
- **Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) on ECG** → the heart pumps against high afterload → myocyte hypertrophy → strain pattern.
- **Retinal arteriolar narrowing** → chronic high pressure causes intimal thickening and arteriolosclerosis.
- **Proteinuria on dip** → glomerular capillary hypertension leads to endothelial damage and leak.

**Connection to pattern recognition cues:**
- The asymptomatic patient with gradual BP rise over years fits the slow, silent pathophysiology.
- Malignant hypertension (atypical) represents acute endothelial injury from very high pressure – fibrinoid necrosis of arterioles, causing AKI, retinopathy, encephalopathy. That is NOT essential disease; it’s decompensated chronic hypertension or a secondary crisis.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Essential (primary) hypertension | Most common; age >40, obesity, family history. No single reversible cause. |
| **Renal** | Chronic kidney disease, renovascular disease (atherosclerotic or fibromuscular dysplasia), polycystic kidneys | Young woman with abdominal bruit (fibromuscular); known CKD; sudden worsening of BP; flash pulmonary oedema (renovascular). |
| **Endocrine** | Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn’s syndrome), phaeochromocytoma, Cushing’s syndrome, hyperthyroidism | Resistant hypertension + hypokalaemia (Conn’s); episodic headaches/palpitations/sweating (phaeo); cushingoid appearance; weight loss/tachycardia (hyperthyroid). |
| **Obstructive sleep apnoea** | OSA causing nocturnal hypoxia → sympathetic activation | Loud snoring, daytime sleepiness, obesity; BP often remains high at night (non-dipper). |
| **Drugs** | NSAIDs, corticosteroids, oral contraceptive pill, liquorice, decongestants | Start of new medication; resistant hypertension; known substance use. |
| **Pregnancy-related** | Pre-eclampsia | Hypertension + proteinuria after 20 weeks (not essential, but important to differentiate). |

**Most common cause:** Essential hypertension (no secondary cause found). Always look for stem clues that point to secondary causes when patient is young (<40), has sudden onset, drug-resistant hypertension, or atypical features.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
The diagnosis of hypertension is based on repeated, standardised office BP measurements:  
- **Stage 1:** ≥140/90 mmHg (clinic) or ≥135/85 mmHg (home/ABPM)  
- **Stage 2:** ≥160/100 mmHg  
- **Severe:** ≥180/120 mmHg (may be emergency)  
*Note:* NICE recommends ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) or home BP monitoring for confirmation of raised clinic readings.

**Pattern-recognition: what confirms?**  
A single BP reading of ≥180/120 mmHg with papilloedema or acute organ damage = hypertensive emergency. Not just hypertension.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Clinic BP** | Resting office BP | Initial screening, then monitoring | Use correct technique. >140/90 → start assessment. |
| **ABPM** | Average BP over 24h (including nocturnal) | Confirm diagnosis; exclude white-coat/masked hypertension | Mean BP ≥135/85 = hypertension. Non-dipping (drop <10% at night) suggests OSA or secondary cause. |
| **Urine dipstick** | Protein, blood, glucose | Screen for renal disease, pre-eclampsia | 1+ protein → microalbuminuria; 2+ → investigate further. |
| **U&Es + eGFR** | Sodium, potassium, creatinine, estimated GFR | Renal function, hyperaldosteronism (low K+), CKD | K+ <3.5 → suspect Conn’s. eGFR <60 → CKD. |
| **Bloods: lipids, glucose** | Cholesterol, HbA1c | Cardiovascular risk assessment | Part of QRISK3 calculation; diabetes doubles risk. |
| **ECG** | LVH, ischaemia, atrial fibrillation | Target organ damage; assess AF risk | LVH by Sokolow-Lyon (SV1+RV5>35mm) or Cornell voltage; Q waves, ST changes. |
| **Fundoscopy** | Retinopathy grade (Keith-Wagener-Barker) | Acute vs chronic; emergency severity | Grade 1: arteriolar narrowing; Grade 2: AV nipping; Grade 3: haemorrhages, exudates; Grade 4: papilloedema. |
| **Additional (if secondary suspected)** | e.g., aldosterone:renin ratio, CT renal arteries, metanephrines | Young, resistant, atypical | Aldosterone:renin >30 suggests Conn’s. Bruit + asymmetric kidneys = renovascular. |
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour)** – *only in hypertensive emergency (BP≥180/120 with organ damage)*
- **What:** Transfer to high-dependency/resus, IV antihypertensives with careful monitoring.
- **Why:** Rapid but controlled lowering of BP (by max 20–25% in first hour) to prevent end-organ infarction (brain, heart, kidney).
- **Drug:** **Labetalol 20 mg IV** over 2 minutes, then 20–80 mg boluses q10–15min to target, or infusion 2 mg/min titrated. Alternative: **Nicardipine infusion 5–15 mg/h**.
- **Do NOT:** Use sublingual nifedipine – causes unpredictable rapid drop → stroke/ischaemia. Avoid clonidine in emergency.

**Ward (24–72 hours)** – *For uncomplicated hypertension (no acute organ damage)*
- **What:** Start oral therapy if BP consistently ≥140/90 (stage 1 with CV risk, or stage 2+). Optimise lifestyle.
- **Why:** Chronic BP lowering reduces long-term events. Start with one drug at low dose.
- **Drug first-line:** **ACE inhibitor or ARB** (if <55y or diabetic) OR **Calcium channel blocker** (if ≥55y or African-Caribbean).  
  Example: **Ramipril 2.5 mg once daily**, or **Amlodipine 5 mg once daily**.
- **Why the age/ethnic split:** Younger Caucasians tend to have high-renin hypertension (ACEi works better); older and African-Caribbean populations have lower renin, so CCB or thiazide more effective.
- **Do NOT:** Start with beta-blocker as first-line (metabolic side effects, less stroke prevention); use only in specific indications (e.g., young patients with sympathetic overdrive, heart failure, migraine).
- **Lifestyle:** Salt <6g/day, DASH diet (low saturated fat, high fruit/veg), reduce alcohol <14 units/week, exercise 30min 5x/week, weight loss, stop smoking.

**Long-term (months to years)**
- **What:** Add or titrate drugs to maintain target BP. Target: <130/80 for most; <120/70 if frailty or high risk?
- **Why:** Two or three drug combinations often needed.
- **Strategy:** If BP not controlled: add second drug from alternative class. If still not controlled: add **thiazide diuretic** (e.g., **Indapamide 2.5 mg once daily**). Triple therapy: ACEi/ARB + CCB + thiazide.
- **If resistant (≥3 drugs at max dose + diuretic):** Check adherence, review secondary causes, consider **spironolactone 25–50 mg once daily** (esp. if K+ normal).
- **Do NOT:** Stop therapy abruptly – risk of rebound hypertension. Taper if needed.
- **Common error:** Escalating the same class instead of adding complementary mechanism.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Essential Hypertension | Secondary – Renal Artery Stenosis | Secondary – Primary Hyperaldosteronism | White-Coat Hypertension |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Onset** | Gradual, after age 40 | Sudden or worsening of established HTN (or onset <30) | Gradual, often resistant | Fluctuating, high in clinic, normal at home |
| **Pressure pattern** | Sustained | Unilateral RAS: severe, resistant; flash pulmonary oedema | Resistant, often high systolic | Only in clinic; ABPM normal |
| **Hypokalaemia** | Rare (unless on diuretic) | No | Yes (spontaneous or easily induced) | No |
| **Abdominal bruit** | No | Yes (systolic-diastolic, paraumbilical) | No | No |
| **Urine dip** | Usually normal | Proteinuria may appear late | Normal | Normal |
| **Renin/aldosterone** | Normal or low renin | Renal artery stenosis: high renin, low aldosterone (unilateral) or high (bilateral) | High aldosterone, low renin (ratio >30) | Normal |
| **Key differentiate** | No secondary cause found | CT angiogram / MRA – renal artery stenosis | Aldosterone:renin ratio, CT adrenals | ABPM or home BP DURING the visit |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Hypertensive emergency / urgency** – rapid BP rise (≥180/120) with end-organ damage. Presents with headache (encephalopathy), blurred vision (retinopathy), dyspnoea (pulmonary oedema), chest pain (aortic dissection). **Key signs:** papilloedema, focal neurology, raised creatinine, raised troponin. **Do:** IV antihypertensives, admit to HDU/ITU, investigate cause.

- **Stroke** – both ischaemic and haemorrhagic. Hypertension is the strongest risk factor. Chronic small vessel disease leads to lacunar infarcts; uncontrolled pressure can rupture Charcot-Bouchard aneurysms causing intracerebral haemorrhage. **Missed?** A patient with “silent” hypertension may present with sudden hemiparesis.

- **Myocardial infarction** – hypertension accelerates atherosclerosis; increased afterload → LVH → increased oxygen demand → ischaemia. **Late presentation:** post-MI cardiogenic shock or heart failure.

- **Chronic kidney disease** – nephrosclerosis. Starts with microalbuminuria, progresses to reduced eGFR. **Late:** uraemia, need for dialysis. *Stem:* “patient with long-standing HTN now has rising creatinine.”

- **Left ventricular hypertrophy** – eventually → diastolic heart failure (heart failure with preserved ejection fraction). **Signs:** SOB, raised JVP, Oedema. **ECG:** LVH strain. **Echo:** thick septum, impaired relaxation.

- **Aortic dissection** – tearing chest pain radiating to back, pulse deficit, wide mediastinum. Immediate hypertensive crisis. **Missed?** Could be misdiagnosed as ACS; always check CXR (widening) and CT.

- **Retinopathy** – from reversible arteriolar narrowing to irreversible exudates, haemorrhages, papilloedema. **Late:** blindness.

- **Dementia** – small vessel disease → vascular dementia.

**Late-presentation pattern:** “A 70-year-old woman with untreated HTN for 20 years presents with dyspnoea and ankle swelling. BP 170/100, JVP +6, bibasal crackles, peripheral oedema. ECG shows LVH and atrial fibrillation.” This is decompensated heart failure from untreated hypertension.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **“ABCDE” for initial management of hypertension:**
  - **A** = Alcohol reduction (<14 units/wk)
  - **B** = Blood pressure reduction (drug + lifestyle)
  - **C** = Cholesterol and CV risk assessment (statins if QRISK3 >10%)
  - **D** = Diet (low salt, DASH)
  - **E** = Exercise (30 min most days)

- **“STOP HTN” for secondary causes:**
  - **S** = Sleep apnoea (OSA)
  - **T** = Thyroid disease (hyperthyroid, Cushing’s)
  - **O** = Obstructive renal artery (RAS, fibromuscular)
  - **P** = Phaeochromocytoma / Primary hyperaldosteronism

**Clinical Pearls:**

1. **The “dipper” pattern** – nocturnal BP normally falls 10–20%. Non-dippers (especially in OSA) are at higher CV risk. Always check ABPM if BP remains high at night.
2. **Resistant hypertension → think spironolactone** – even if no hyperaldosteronism, spironolactone often works when triple therapy fails.
3. **Don’t treat the number alone** – treat the patient. A frail 90-year-old with BP 150/90 may not need medication; focus on reducing falls risk.
4. **Postural hypotension** – common in elderly with hypertension, especially on multiple drugs. Check lying and standing BP. If standing drops >20 mmHg, reduce or stop medication.
5. **Beta-blockers are not first-line** – but they are excellent if the patient has migraine, tremor, or arrhythmia.
6. **ACEi/ARB and pregnancy** – contraindicated (fetotoxic). In pregnant patients with hypertension, use labetalol, nifedipine, or methyldopa.
7. **Measure BP in BOTH arms** – >20 mmHg difference suggests subclavian stenosis or coarctation.

**Common mistakes:**
- Using a cuff that is too small (overestimates BP by up to 10-15 mmHg).
- Diagnosing from one reading in a stressed patient.
- Stopping treatment once BP normalises – it normalises BECAUSE of medication.
- Prescribing ACEi without checking K+ and creatinine, especially if starting a diuretic at the same time.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**Call senior / acute medical team if:**

- **Hypertensive emergency** – BP ≥180/120 mmHg with any of: papilloedema, retinal haemorrhages, acute kidney injury (creatinine rise), acute heart failure/pulmonary oedema, hypertensive encephalopathy (altered GCS, seizures), acute stroke, acute coronary syndrome, aortic dissection.
- **NEWS2** – any single physiological parameter deviation: e.g., RR >24/min, SpO2 <92%, HR >130 or <40, SBP <90 or >220.
- **Lactate** – >2.0 mmol/L (especially if rising).
- **GCS** – <15 or falling, especially if no focal neurology (think encephalopathy).
- **Potassium** – <3.0 or >6.5 (caution with spironolactone/ACEi).
- **eGFR** – acute drop >30% from baseline (stop ACEi/ARB, assess volume status).
- **Atrial fibrillation** – new onset (rate control needed; anticoagulation if CHA₂DS₂-VASc >1 in men, >2 in women).
- **Suspected phaeochromocytoma** – paroxysmal hypertension, headache, pallor, sweating → urgent metanephrines, alpha-blockade before any intervention.

**Call ICU if:**
- Required IV antihypertensives for ongoing control (labetalol infusion, nicardipine)
- Hypertensive encephalopathy with depressed GCS
- Pulmonary oedema requiring non-invasive ventilation
- Aortic dissection (type A → emergent surgery)
- Need for central line, arterial line monitoring
- Multiple organ failure

**Key thresholds summary:**
- **SBP >220 or DBP >120 mmHg** → start IV therapy, admit to monitored bed.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** → clinical emergency, senior review immediately, consider level 2/3 care.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** → sepsis? hypoperfusion? (not typical for simple hypertension, but possible in emergency)
- **GCS ≤12** → airway and CT brain before BP lowering (rule out bleed).

Always remember: **The decision to escalate is not just the number – it’s the number PLUS organ dysfunction. A BP of 200/110 in an asymptomatic patient with normal exam and labs can be managed orally – treat the patient, not the screen.**

rawMarkdown:
## Essential Hypertension — Study Mode

**One-liner:** Chronic elevation of systemic arterial pressure (≥140/90 mmHg) without a single identifiable cause; affects ~1 in 3 adults; major preventable risk for stroke, MI, kidney failure.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 52-year-old man attends a routine health screen. He is asymptomatic, works as a taxi driver, smokes 20/day, and drinks 6 units of alcohol nightly. His BMI is 30. Blood pressure is 162/98 in both arms; his heart rate is 78 bpm. Cardiorespiratory exam is normal, no renal bruit, fundi show no papilloedema. Urine dipstick – nil. He has no known comorbidities. You check his previous records: BP was 145/90 two years ago but not rechecked.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
- **Asymptomatic** → essential hypertension is usually silent; symptoms only appear with target-organ damage or very high readings.
- **Raised BP in both arms** → excludes coarctation when difference is absent (≥20 mmHg suggests coarctation).
- **Obesity, smoking, alcohol** → classic lifestyle drivers; essential hypertension clusters with metabolic syndrome.
- **Normal fundi** → no hypertensive retinopathy (yet); this is chronic, not acute emergency.
- **No renal bruit** → unlikely renovascular hypertension.
- **Normal urine dip** → no glomerular disease or proteinuria.
- **Previous borderline readings** → confirms chronicity; this is essential (primary) hypertension, not an acute stress response.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Malignant hypertension** – young woman (20s–30s) with headache, blurred vision, BP 230/130, acute kidney injury, papilloedema. This is NOT essential disease; it’s a hypertensive emergency requiring immediate IV therapy.
- **Labile hypertension** – patient with normal clinic BP but very high home readings (or vice versa). May be white-coat effect or masked hypertension (ambulatory BP monitoring needed).
- **Isolated systolic hypertension** – elderly patient with systolic >140 but diastolic <90. Often stiff arteries due to atherosclerosis; still requires treatment to prevent stroke.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Essential (or primary) hypertension is a sustained increase in the pressure of blood against artery walls that has no single, reversible medical cause. It results from a complex interplay of genetics, sodium intake, age, and lifestyle factors over years. It is by far the most common type of hypertension (90–95% of cases).

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** 1 in 3 adults in the UK has hypertension; prevalence rises with age to >60% over 60 years. It is the most common chronic condition in primary care.
- **Stakes:** Untreated hypertension is the single largest modifiable risk factor for stroke, myocardial infarction, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease. It kills silently.
- **Common pitfalls:** (1) Failing to measure BP correctly – patient should be seated, arm supported, cuff size appropriate; (2) diagnosing from one reading – needs confirmation over visits; (3) stopping treatment once BP “normalises” – hypertension is chronic, medication is usually lifelong; (4) ignoring secondary causes in young or treatment-resistant patients.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:**  
Essential hypertension is a slow, multi-system failure of blood pressure regulation. Think of it as the arterial tree “hardening” and volume control becoming less efficient.

- **Cardiac output (CO) and systemic vascular resistance (SVR)** – Blood pressure = CO × SVR. In early essential hypertension, CO is often normal or slightly elevated (sympathetic overdrive). Over time, the kidneys fail to excrete sodium properly, leading to subtle volume expansion. Arterioles remodel and vasoconstrict (increased SVR). This pushes BP up.

- **Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS)** – In essential hypertension, the RAAS is often inappropriately active for the salt intake. High renin → angiotensin II (powerful vasoconstrictor and stimulator of aldosterone) → sodium and water retention. The kidney’s pressure-natriuresis curve shifts right: you need higher BP to excrete the same sodium load.

- **Sympathetic nervous system** – Overactivity (from stress, obesity, sleep apnoea) causes direct vasoconstriction and tachycardia. This explains why some patients have a high heart rate and why lifestyle modification helps.

- **Endothelial dysfunction** – Nitric oxide production decreases; vessels lose their ability to dilate. This contributes to the increased SVR and explains why antihypertensives that enhance NO (like ACE inhibitors) work.

- **Sodium and volume** – High dietary sodium → increased plasma volume → higher cardiac output → chronic adaptation: autoregulation increases SVR to normalise flow at higher pressure. Once established, the hypertension is volume-dependent in some patients (salt-sensitive).

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Elevated BP on measurement** → direct result of increased SVR and/or CO.
- **Usually asymptomatic** → the body adapts slowly; organ damage takes years.
- **Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) on ECG** → the heart pumps against high afterload → myocyte hypertrophy → strain pattern.
- **Retinal arteriolar narrowing** → chronic high pressure causes intimal thickening and arteriolosclerosis.
- **Proteinuria on dip** → glomerular capillary hypertension leads to endothelial damage and leak.

**Connection to pattern recognition cues:**
- The asymptomatic patient with gradual BP rise over years fits the slow, silent pathophysiology.
- Malignant hypertension (atypical) represents acute endothelial injury from very high pressure – fibrinoid necrosis of arterioles, causing AKI, retinopathy, encephalopathy. That is NOT essential disease; it’s decompensated chronic hypertension or a secondary crisis.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Essential (primary) hypertension | Most common; age >40, obesity, family history. No single reversible cause. |
| **Renal** | Chronic kidney disease, renovascular disease (atherosclerotic or fibromuscular dysplasia), polycystic kidneys | Young woman with abdominal bruit (fibromuscular); known CKD; sudden worsening of BP; flash pulmonary oedema (renovascular). |
| **Endocrine** | Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn’s syndrome), phaeochromocytoma, Cushing’s syndrome, hyperthyroidism | Resistant hypertension + hypokalaemia (Conn’s); episodic headaches/palpitations/sweating (phaeo); cushingoid appearance; weight loss/tachycardia (hyperthyroid). |
| **Obstructive sleep apnoea** | OSA causing nocturnal hypoxia → sympathetic activation | Loud snoring, daytime sleepiness, obesity; BP often remains high at night (non-dipper). |
| **Drugs** | NSAIDs, corticosteroids, oral contraceptive pill, liquorice, decongestants | Start of new medication; resistant hypertension; known substance use. |
| **Pregnancy-related** | Pre-eclampsia | Hypertension + proteinuria after 20 weeks (not essential, but important to differentiate). |

**Most common cause:** Essential hypertension (no secondary cause found). Always look for stem clues that point to secondary causes when patient is young (<40), has sudden onset, drug-resistant hypertension, or atypical features.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
The diagnosis of hypertension is based on repeated, standardised office BP measurements:  
- **Stage 1:** ≥140/90 mmHg (clinic) or ≥135/85 mmHg (home/ABPM)  
- **Stage 2:** ≥160/100 mmHg  
- **Severe:** ≥180/120 mmHg (may be emergency)  
*Note:* NICE recommends ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) or home BP monitoring for confirmation of raised clinic readings.

**Pattern-recognition: what confirms?**  
A single BP reading of ≥180/120 mmHg with papilloedema or acute organ damage = hypertensive emergency. Not just hypertension.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Clinic BP** | Resting office BP | Initial screening, then monitoring | Use correct technique. >140/90 → start assessment. |
| **ABPM** | Average BP over 24h (including nocturnal) | Confirm diagnosis; exclude white-coat/masked hypertension | Mean BP ≥135/85 = hypertension. Non-dipping (drop <10% at night) suggests OSA or secondary cause. |
| **Urine dipstick** | Protein, blood, glucose | Screen for renal disease, pre-eclampsia | 1+ protein → microalbuminuria; 2+ → investigate further. |
| **U&Es + eGFR** | Sodium, potassium, creatinine, estimated GFR | Renal function, hyperaldosteronism (low K+), CKD | K+ <3.5 → suspect Conn’s. eGFR <60 → CKD. |
| **Bloods: lipids, glucose** | Cholesterol, HbA1c | Cardiovascular risk assessment | Part of QRISK3 calculation; diabetes doubles risk. |
| **ECG** | LVH, ischaemia, atrial fibrillation | Target organ damage; assess AF risk | LVH by Sokolow-Lyon (SV1+RV5>35mm) or Cornell voltage; Q waves, ST changes. |
| **Fundoscopy** | Retinopathy grade (Keith-Wagener-Barker) | Acute vs chronic; emergency severity | Grade 1: arteriolar narrowing; Grade 2: AV nipping; Grade 3: haemorrhages, exudates; Grade 4: papilloedema. |
| **Additional (if secondary suspected)** | e.g., aldosterone:renin ratio, CT renal arteries, metanephrines | Young, resistant, atypical | Aldosterone:renin >30 suggests Conn’s. Bruit + asymmetric kidneys = renovascular. |

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour)** – *only in hypertensive emergency (BP≥180/120 with organ damage)*
- **What:** Transfer to high-dependency/resus, IV antihypertensives with careful monitoring.
- **Why:** Rapid but controlled lowering of BP (by max 20–25% in first hour) to prevent end-organ infarction (brain, heart, kidney).
- **Drug:** **Labetalol 20 mg IV** over 2 minutes, then 20–80 mg boluses q10–15min to target, or infusion 2 mg/min titrated. Alternative: **Nicardipine infusion 5–15 mg/h**.
- **Do NOT:** Use sublingual nifedipine – causes unpredictable rapid drop → stroke/ischaemia. Avoid clonidine in emergency.

**Ward (24–72 hours)** – *For uncomplicated hypertension (no acute organ damage)*
- **What:** Start oral therapy if BP consistently ≥140/90 (stage 1 with CV risk, or stage 2+). Optimise lifestyle.
- **Why:** Chronic BP lowering reduces long-term events. Start with one drug at low dose.
- **Drug first-line:** **ACE inhibitor or ARB** (if <55y or diabetic) OR **Calcium channel blocker** (if ≥55y or African-Caribbean).  
  Example: **Ramipril 2.5 mg once daily**, or **Amlodipine 5 mg once daily**.
- **Why the age/ethnic split:** Younger Caucasians tend to have high-renin hypertension (ACEi works better); older and African-Caribbean populations have lower renin, so CCB or thiazide more effective.
- **Do NOT:** Start with beta-blocker as first-line (metabolic side effects, less stroke prevention); use only in specific indications (e.g., young patients with sympathetic overdrive, heart failure, migraine).
- **Lifestyle:** Salt <6g/day, DASH diet (low saturated fat, high fruit/veg), reduce alcohol <14 units/week, exercise 30min 5x/week, weight loss, stop smoking.

**Long-term (months to years)**
- **What:** Add or titrate drugs to maintain target BP. Target: <130/80 for most; <120/70 if frailty or high risk?
- **Why:** Two or three drug combinations often needed.
- **Strategy:** If BP not controlled: add second drug from alternative class. If still not controlled: add **thiazide diuretic** (e.g., **Indapamide 2.5 mg once daily**). Triple therapy: ACEi/ARB + CCB + thiazide.
- **If resistant (≥3 drugs at max dose + diuretic):** Check adherence, review secondary causes, consider **spironolactone 25–50 mg once daily** (esp. if K+ normal).
- **Do NOT:** Stop therapy abruptly – risk of rebound hypertension. Taper if needed.
- **Common error:** Escalating the same class instead of adding complementary mechanism.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Essential Hypertension | Secondary – Renal Artery Stenosis | Secondary – Primary Hyperaldosteronism | White-Coat Hypertension |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Onset** | Gradual, after age 40 | Sudden or worsening of established HTN (or onset <30) | Gradual, often resistant | Fluctuating, high in clinic, normal at home |
| **Pressure pattern** | Sustained | Unilateral RAS: severe, resistant; flash pulmonary oedema | Resistant, often high systolic | Only in clinic; ABPM normal |
| **Hypokalaemia** | Rare (unless on diuretic) | No | Yes (spontaneous or easily induced) | No |
| **Abdominal bruit** | No | Yes (systolic-diastolic, paraumbilical) | No | No |
| **Urine dip** | Usually normal | Proteinuria may appear late | Normal | Normal |
| **Renin/aldosterone** | Normal or low renin | Renal artery stenosis: high renin, low aldosterone (unilateral) or high (bilateral) | High aldosterone, low renin (ratio >30) | Normal |
| **Key differentiate** | No secondary cause found | CT angiogram / MRA – renal artery stenosis | Aldosterone:renin ratio, CT adrenals | ABPM or home BP DURING the visit |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Hypertensive emergency / urgency** – rapid BP rise (≥180/120) with end-organ damage. Presents with headache (encephalopathy), blurred vision (retinopathy), dyspnoea (pulmonary oedema), chest pain (aortic dissection). **Key signs:** papilloedema, focal neurology, raised creatinine, raised troponin. **Do:** IV antihypertensives, admit to HDU/ITU, investigate cause.

- **Stroke** – both ischaemic and haemorrhagic. Hypertension is the strongest risk factor. Chronic small vessel disease leads to lacunar infarcts; uncontrolled pressure can rupture Charcot-Bouchard aneurysms causing intracerebral haemorrhage. **Missed?** A patient with “silent” hypertension may present with sudden hemiparesis.

- **Myocardial infarction** – hypertension accelerates atherosclerosis; increased afterload → LVH → increased oxygen demand → ischaemia. **Late presentation:** post-MI cardiogenic shock or heart failure.

- **Chronic kidney disease** – nephrosclerosis. Starts with microalbuminuria, progresses to reduced eGFR. **Late:** uraemia, need for dialysis. *Stem:* “patient with long-standing HTN now has rising creatinine.”

- **Left ventricular hypertrophy** – eventually → diastolic heart failure (heart failure with preserved ejection fraction). **Signs:** SOB, raised JVP, Oedema. **ECG:** LVH strain. **Echo:** thick septum, impaired relaxation.

- **Aortic dissection** – tearing chest pain radiating to back, pulse deficit, wide mediastinum. Immediate hypertensive crisis. **Missed?** Could be misdiagnosed as ACS; always check CXR (widening) and CT.

- **Retinopathy** – from reversible arteriolar narrowing to irreversible exudates, haemorrhages, papilloedema. **Late:** blindness.

- **Dementia** – small vessel disease → vascular dementia.

**Late-presentation pattern:** “A 70-year-old woman with untreated HTN for 20 years presents with dyspnoea and ankle swelling. BP 170/100, JVP +6, bibasal crackles, peripheral oedema. ECG shows LVH and atrial fibrillation.” This is decompensated heart failure from untreated hypertension.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **“ABCDE” for initial management of hypertension:**
  - **A** = Alcohol reduction (<14 units/wk)
  - **B** = Blood pressure reduction (drug + lifestyle)
  - **C** = Cholesterol and CV risk assessment (statins if QRISK3 >10%)
  - **D** = Diet (low salt, DASH)
  - **E** = Exercise (30 min most days)

- **“STOP HTN” for secondary causes:**
  - **S** = Sleep apnoea (OSA)
  - **T** = Thyroid disease (hyperthyroid, Cushing’s)
  - **O** = Obstructive renal artery (RAS, fibromuscular)
  - **P** = Phaeochromocytoma / Primary hyperaldosteronism

**Clinical Pearls:**

1. **The “dipper” pattern** – nocturnal BP normally falls 10–20%. Non-dippers (especially in OSA) are at higher CV risk. Always check ABPM if BP remains high at night.
2. **Resistant hypertension → think spironolactone** – even if no hyperaldosteronism, spironolactone often works when triple therapy fails.
3. **Don’t treat the number alone** – treat the patient. A frail 90-year-old with BP 150/90 may not need medication; focus on reducing falls risk.
4. **Postural hypotension** – common in elderly with hypertension, especially on multiple drugs. Check lying and standing BP. If standing drops >20 mmHg, reduce or stop medication.
5. **Beta-blockers are not first-line** – but they are excellent if the patient has migraine, tremor, or arrhythmia.
6. **ACEi/ARB and pregnancy** – contraindicated (fetotoxic). In pregnant patients with hypertension, use labetalol, nifedipine, or methyldopa.
7. **Measure BP in BOTH arms** – >20 mmHg difference suggests subclavian stenosis or coarctation.

**Common mistakes:**
- Using a cuff that is too small (overestimates BP by up to 10-15 mmHg).
- Diagnosing from one reading in a stressed patient.
- Stopping treatment once BP normalises – it normalises BECAUSE of medication.
- Prescribing ACEi without checking K+ and creatinine, especially if starting a diuretic at the same time.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**Call senior / acute medical team if:**

- **Hypertensive emergency** – BP ≥180/120 mmHg with any of: papilloedema, retinal haemorrhages, acute kidney injury (creatinine rise), acute heart failure/pulmonary oedema, hypertensive encephalopathy (altered GCS, seizures), acute stroke, acute coronary syndrome, aortic dissection.
- **NEWS2** – any single physiological parameter deviation: e.g., RR >24/min, SpO2 <92%, HR >130 or <40, SBP <90 or >220.
- **Lactate** – >2.0 mmol/L (especially if rising).
- **GCS** – <15 or falling, especially if no focal neurology (think encephalopathy).
- **Potassium** – <3.0 or >6.5 (caution with spironolactone/ACEi).
- **eGFR** – acute drop >30% from baseline (stop ACEi/ARB, assess volume status).
- **Atrial fibrillation** – new onset (rate control needed; anticoagulation if CHA₂DS₂-VASc >1 in men, >2 in women).
- **Suspected phaeochromocytoma** – paroxysmal hypertension, headache, pallor, sweating → urgent metanephrines, alpha-blockade before any intervention.

**Call ICU if:**
- Required IV antihypertensives for ongoing control (labetalol infusion, nicardipine)
- Hypertensive encephalopathy with depressed GCS
- Pulmonary oedema requiring non-invasive ventilation
- Aortic dissection (type A → emergent surgery)
- Need for central line, arterial line monitoring
- Multiple organ failure

**Key thresholds summary:**
- **SBP >220 or DBP >120 mmHg** → start IV therapy, admit to monitored bed.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** → clinical emergency, senior review immediately, consider level 2/3 care.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** → sepsis? hypoperfusion? (not typical for simple hypertension, but possible in emergency)
- **GCS ≤12** → airway and CT brain before BP lowering (rule out bleed).

Always remember: **The decision to escalate is not just the number – it’s the number PLUS organ dysfunction. A BP of 200/110 in an asymptomatic patient with normal exam and labs can be managed orally – treat the patient, not the screen.**
